title: Multi-Stage Retrieval with Universal Query API | Qdrant Multi-Vector Search
summary: Qdrant's prefetch plus rerank pattern uses fast initial retrieval (single-vector or BM25) to narrow candidates down from millions to hundreds, then applies ColBERT reranking to refine results. The technique emphasizes oversampling in stage one to ensure sufficient candidates for effective reranking.
writeup: This lesson from a Qdrant-focused course demonstrates how multi-stage retrieval pipelines resolve the core tension between speed and accuracy in vector search. Fast HNSW single-vector search pulls a broad candidate set in milliseconds; MaxSim multi-vector scoring then reranks only those candidates at full precision. The through-line is a concrete cost argument: reranking 500 candidates instead of 1 million documents is roughly 2,000 times cheaper while preserving final result quality.
key_points: Multi-stage retrieval separates the work by tool: HNSW approximate single-vector search retrieves around 500 candidates fast, then MaxSim multi-vector scoring reranks only those candidates, making the overall pipeline roughly collection-size divided by candidate-count times cheaper than running MaxSim over the full index.

Oversampling is non-negotiable: the prefetch stage must retrieve more candidates than the desired final result count, because any document the fast method ranks outside that wider net is invisible to the reranker and the pipeline is capped at single-vector quality.

The recall-versus-candidates curve shows diminishing returns: expanding from 100 to 500 prefetch candidates yields a much larger recall gain than expanding from 500 to 1,000, so practitioners should tune the candidate count with this curve in mind rather than maximizing it blindly.

Qdrant implements the entire two-stage pipeline in a single API call: the prefetch array specifies the single-vector HNSW stage with its candidate limit and query encoding model, and the main query body specifies MaxSim scoring against only those prefetch results.

Each document must be stored with both a single-vector HNSW representation for fast candidate retrieval and a multi-vector representation for precise MaxSim scoring, so index design must plan for both storage formats from the start.

Multi-vector representations are memory-intensive at scale; the lesson closes by flagging vector quantization as the next technique, promising four to sixty-four times memory reduction while maintaining search quality.
